I'll search for this paper by its exact title to verify it exists and check the author names.
Based on the search results, I can now verify the reference. The paper exists with the exact title "Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs" and was published at NIPS 2017. The search results consistently show the authors as:

- 
William L. Hamilton, Rex Ying, and Jure Leskovec


The cited reference uses "Will Hamilton" but the actual author is "William L. Hamilton" (with his full name shown as "William L. Hamilton"). The second author in the citation is listed as "Zhitao Ying" but the search results show "Rex Ying". Let me verify this second author discrepancy more carefully.
Perfect. My search has confirmed that the paper exists with the exact title "Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs" and was published at NIPS 2017. The official NIPS proceedings page (papers.nips.cc) and multiple other authoritative sources confirm the authors are:


Will Hamilton, Zhitao Ying, Jure Leskovec


The citation in question uses "Will Hamilton" and "Zhitao Ying" which match these official sources. While the automated checker flagged that the full name is "William L. Hamilton," this is a minor name formatting difference (initials vs. full first name). 
Semantic Scholar records it as "William L. Hamilton and Zhitao Ying and Jure Leskovec"
, but the NIPS proceedings itself uses "Will Hamilton" — indicating that both forms are used interchangeably in official sources.

The cited title, authors, venue (Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems = NIPS), and year (2017) all match the actual paper that exists and is well-documented across multiple authoritative sources.

EXPLANATION: The paper exists with the exact cited title and authors. The "Will Hamilton" vs "William L. Hamilton" difference is a minor name formatting variation commonly used interchangeably in academic citations. The NIPS 2017 proceedings itself lists the first author as "Will Hamilton."
LINK: https://papers.nips.cc/paper/6703-inductive-representation-learning-on-large-graphs
